title: Survivorship Curve Explorer
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive graph comparing Type I, II, and III survivorship curves with real species data overlays and hover-to-reveal demographic details

educational.topic: Survivorship Curves and Life History Strategies
learningObjectives: Compare the three survivorship curve types and match real species to each pattern
Explain the relationship between reproductive strategy and survivorship pattern
prerequisites: Understanding of population concepts (birth rate, death rate)
Introduction to r-selected and K-selected species

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Interactive exploration with species comparison and quantitative data extraction
keyQuestions: Why do some species produce thousands of offspring while others produce very few?
What does a constant mortality rate look like on a survivorship graph?
How do parental care and offspring number represent a trade-off?
Which survivorship type is most common in nature, and why?
commonMisconceptions: Students often think Type III species are less successful than Type I, not realizing both strategies are equally valid evolutionary solutions
Students may confuse survivorship curves with population growth curves
Students sometimes think all mammals are Type I, when some small mammals are closer to Type II
assessmentOpportunities: Have students fill in a data table by hovering over curves at specific lifespan percentages
Ask students to predict survivorship curve types for novel species and justify their reasoning
Students compare human survivorship curves across historical time periods or countries
